title: Add Redis-backed rate limiting to the public API
summary: Introduces a per-client, per-minute request ceiling on the public API, enforced by a new Rack middleware backed by Redis counters. Each API client gets a tier (standard / pro / enterprise) with a sensible default, plus an optional per-client override for noisy-but-trusted integrations. Over-limit requests get a 429 with Retry-After; every response advertises the client's remaining budget via X-RateLimit-* headers.

start_here.reason: schema change — defines the tier + override columns everything else reads (+7/-0)
the enforcement path — where a request can be turned into a 429 (+57/-0)
defines how a client's effective limit is computed (+22/-0)

chapters.title: Data model & migrations
chapters.summary: Adds rate_limit_tier and requests_per_minute to api_clients, with an index on the tier.
chapters.intent: Give every API client a durable tier and an optional per-client ceiling, so the limit travels with the record instead of living only in config.

diff_hunks.summary: Two non-null columns with defaults + a tier index.

review_notes: Both columns are NOT NULL with defaults, so existing rows backfill automatically — confirm 'standard'/60 is the right default for clients created before this change.
On a large api_clients table, adding NOT NULL columns can lock writes; is this table small enough, or should the column be added nullable then backfilled?
There's no down-migration override — verify `change` reverses cleanly (drops both columns and the index).
verification_steps: Run the migration forward, then `rails db:rollback STEP=1`, on a copy of production.
Confirm a pre-existing client loads with rate_limit_tier='standard' and requests_per_minute=60.

chapters.title: Domain model — ApiClient#rate_limit
chapters.summary: Adds the TIER_LIMITS table, a tier validation, the effective-limit calculation, and the Redis key helper.
chapters.intent: Make the model the single source of truth for 'what is this client's limit right now', so the middleware stays dumb and testable.

diff_hunks.summary: Tier constants, validation, rate_limit, and rate_limit_key.

review_notes: rate_limit takes the MAX of the tier default and the override — so the override can only raise the ceiling, never tighten it. Is that the intended policy? A client downgraded to a stricter custom limit would be silently ignored.
`requests_per_minute.to_i` coerces nil to 0, which is harmless given the max(); confirm the column's NOT NULL default makes nil impossible anyway.
rate_limit_key embeds the record id only — fine for a single Redis namespace, but worth confirming keys can't collide across environments sharing one Redis.
verification_steps: In a console: set tier='pro', expect rate_limit == 600; then set requests_per_minute=1000 and expect 1000.
Set an invalid tier and confirm the validation rejects it.

chapters.title: Request middleware — RateLimiter
chapters.summary: New Rack middleware: increments a per-window Redis counter, short-circuits with 429 over the ceiling, and stamps X-RateLimit-* headers on every API response.
chapters.intent: Enforce the ceiling at the edge of the request cycle, before controllers run, while leaving non-API and unauthenticated traffic completely untouched.

diff_hunks.summary: call() throttling path, INCR+EXPIRE round-trip, and the 429 response.

review_notes: The window is a wall-clock minute string (YYYYMMDDHHMM), so limits reset on fixed boundaries rather than a sliding window — a client can burst 2x the ceiling across a boundary. Acceptable for v1?
If Redis is down, @redis.multi raises and the request 500s — should the middleware fail OPEN (allow the request) instead of taking the API down with Redis?
EXPIRE is reset to 60s on every increment, so a continuously-busy key never expires mid-window; confirm that's intended and not a subtle key-leak.
Security-sensitive path: this can hard-block legitimate clients. Double-check api_path? and the `client` guard so a misconfigured route can't throttle the whole app.
verification_steps: Hit an /api/ endpoint as a standard client 61 times in a minute; expect the 61st to return 429 with Retry-After: 60.
Stop Redis and send one API request; observe the failure mode and decide whether it matches the desired open/closed policy.
Confirm a non-/api/ path (e.g. /health) and an unauthenticated request pass through with no X-RateLimit headers.

chapters.title: Configuration
chapters.summary: Adds config/rate_limits.yml: per-environment enable flag and the documented tier ceilings.
chapters.intent: Let enforcement be toggled per environment (off in development) and keep the tier ceilings written down in one place for seeds and humans.
review_notes: The middleware reads ApiClient#rate_limit directly and doesn't appear to consult this file's `enabled` flag — confirm something actually wires `enabled: false` to skip the middleware in development, or the flag is a no-op.
burst_multiplier and retry_after_seconds are declared here but Retry-After is hard-coded to 60 in the middleware; reconcile the two so the config isn't misleading.
verification_steps: Boot in development and confirm API requests are not throttled.
Confirm production tier ceilings here match TIER_LIMITS in the model.

chapters.title: Tests
chapters.summary: Specs for the middleware: under-limit pass-through, 429 over the ceiling, non-API/unauthenticated pass-through, and per-client override.
chapters.intent: Lock in the four behaviors that matter: it throttles, it doesn't over-throttle, it ignores what it should, and overrides win.

diff_hunks.summary: Four examples over a MockRedis-backed middleware.

review_notes: Tests use MockRedis — does it implement multi/incr/expire faithfully enough that a green suite really proves the production Redis path? Consider one integration test against a real Redis.
There's no test for the window boundary (the burst-across-reset case) or for Redis being unavailable — the two riskiest behaviors are currently unspecified.
verification_steps: Run `bundle exec rspec spec/middleware/rate_limiter_spec.rb` and confirm all four examples pass.
Temporarily break rate_limit in the model and confirm the 429 test fails (the test actually exercises the limit).

chapters.title: Documentation
chapters.summary: Adds docs/api/rate-limiting.md: tiers table, override behavior, and the response headers contract.
chapters.intent: Tell API consumers exactly what to expect — the ceilings, how to read their remaining budget, and what a 429 looks like.

diff_hunks.summary: Tiers, override rule, and X-RateLimit-* / 429 contract.

review_notes: The doc promises X-RateLimit-Limit and X-RateLimit-Remaining but not the X-RateLimit-Reset many clients expect — call out its absence or add it.
Confirm the documented ceilings (60 / 600 / 6,000) stay in sync with TIER_LIMITS; this is the third place those numbers appear (model, config, docs).
verification_steps: Render the Markdown and check the table and code spans display correctly.
Cross-check every number and header name against the middleware and model.
